title: Add context-alignment check to skill security audit
description: Extend soul_manager.audit_skill() to check whether a skill's actual operations match its declared purpose (tags/category), not just whether individual operations look suspicious in isolation.
rationale: The reading note's core insight: 'the attack surface is context, not the system — legitimate tools doing legitimate things in the wrong context.' The current audit scans for malicious-looking content (suspicious URLs, eval(), obfuscated code). The note explicitly flags this as insufficient: '现有的 DLP 思路还是在扫内容，而不是扫语境.' A skill tagged [math, proof] that makes external HTTP calls isn't necessarily malicious code — but it is a context mismatch. The trust architecture of the skill (what it claims to be) is itself an attack surface, exactly parallel to the OpenClaw case: not malicious code, just the wrong tool trusted in the wrong place.
diff_description: In soul_manager.audit_skill(), add a _check_context_alignment(skill_tags, skill_code) step after the existing checks. Define a tag-to-allowed-operations map, e.g.: {'math': no_network, no_subprocess}, {'writing': no_subprocess, no_filesystem_outside_sandbox}, {'photo': no_network_except_local}, {'coding': sandbox_filesystem_only}. For each skill, extract which operation classes it uses (network calls via requests/urllib/httpx, subprocess/os.system, filesystem reads outside ~/Sandbox/, etc.) and cross-reference against what its tags permit. Flag as CONTEXT_MISMATCH if a skill's operations exceed its declared scope — even if those operations would pass the existing content-based checks. Log the mismatch with: skill name, declared tags, detected operation classes, and which constraint was violated. This doesn't block the skill automatically (the content checks do that) — it raises a warning that routes to a human review queue or gets logged in the audit trail, so that legitimate-but-misscoped skills are visible rather than silently trusted.
